title: SEGMENT 1 — Why regulation matters as much as the biology — the gauntlet between bench and bedside

lines.text: SEGMENT 1 — Why regulation matters as much as the biology — the gauntlet between bench and bedside
Alright.
Let's get started.

Today, for the next two hours, we are going to talk about something that almost nobody finds glamorous, that almost nobody writes popular books about, and that almost nobody outside of a very specific professional community ever bothers to learn.
But here is the claim I want to make in the first sixty seconds of this lecture, before you have any reason to believe me, and that I will spend the next two hours trying to defend.
The regulatory pathway for a gene therapy is, in its own way, as important to whether that therapy ever reaches a patient as the underlying science.
A perfect molecular insight that cannot navigate the regulatory gauntlet is, clinically, worth nothing.

And a regulatory framework that is too permissive or too restrictive doesn't just slow down a particular drug — it shapes which kinds of diseases get attacked, which patients get treated, and which biotechnology companies survive long enough to bring anything to market at all.
The regulators are not, contrary to the way they are sometimes portrayed in the press, a bureaucratic obstacle dropped on top of the real work.
They are, in a very real sense, co-architects of what modern medicine becomes.
So this lecture is the regulatory tour.
We're going to walk, end to end, through how a gene therapy actually becomes an approved drug in the United States and in the European Union.
We will start at the conceptual stage — somebody has an idea — and we will end at a registry following patients for fifteen years after they were dosed.

Along the way we will meet the agencies — FDA, CBER, OTAT in the US; EMA and CAT in Europe — we will meet the milestones — preclinical, pre-IND, IND, Phase 1, Phase 2, Phase 3, BLA, post-market — and we will meet the gene therapies themselves: Luxturna, Zolgensma, Hemgenix, Casgevy, Lyfgenia.
Each of them has a story about how it cleared the gauntlet, and each of those stories teaches you something a little different about how the system actually works.
Let me give you the analogy that I want you to hold in your head for the entire two hours.
The regulatory pathway is a gauntlet.
Imagine a long hallway with a series of gates in it, each of them locked, each of them opened only when you pass a specific test.
You start at one end of the hallway with an idea.
You end at the other end of the hallway with an approved, marketable drug.
To get from the start to the end, you have to clear every gate in order.

Concept gates open into preclinical gates, which open into IND gates, which open into Phase 1 gates, which open into Phase 2 gates, which open into Phase 3 gates, which open into the BLA gate, which finally opens into approval.
And after approval there is still a kind of long tail of post-market commitments that you carry for years afterwards.
Here is the number I want you to remember from this opening.
About ninety percent of drugs that enter Phase 1 clinical trials never make it to approval.
Ninety percent.

That means for every ten gene therapies that get permission to be tested in a single human being, nine of them, on average, will fail somewhere along the way — fail for safety reasons, fail for efficacy reasons, fail for manufacturing reasons, fail because the company runs out of money, fail because a competitor gets there first, fail because the FDA asks for another trial that the sponsor cannot afford to run.
The gauntlet is not a formality.
It is a winnowing process, and the winnowing is severe.

If you walk out of here in two hours and you can do three things — one, draw the development pipeline on a napkin and explain in plain English what happens at each stage, from preclinical mouse studies through the BLA filing to post-market surveillance; two, name and briefly characterize the four big accelerated pathways the FDA offers — Fast Track, Breakthrough Therapy, RMAT, and Priority Review — and explain why gene therapies in particular tend to qualify for several of them at once; three,
tell me the story of at least one approved AAV gene therapy and at least one CRISPR gene therapy and explain what regulatory choices made each of them possible — then we have succeeded.
Everything else, all the detail about what the agencies look at and how the meetings are structured and what the advisory committees do, hangs off those three.
One more thing before we open the box.
This is a story that has been moving fast.

Most of the major regulatory frameworks for gene therapy that we are going to discuss — the RMAT designation, the CBER reorganization that produced OTAT, the long-term follow-up guidance for AAV vectors, the platform technology designation — these are creations of the last fifteen years.

The framework you are about to learn is not the framework that existed when the first gene therapy trials happened in the 1990s, and it almost certainly is not the framework that will exist twenty years from now.
We are going to spend most of our time on the current state of things, but I want you to hold the historical contingency in mind.
Regulation is a living organism.
It evolves with the science it regulates.
